front: Novel: in this novel, the protagonist gives the name 'Ylajali' to a woman he meets while wandering the streets of Kristiania suffering from starvation
Novel: in this novel, the protagonist repeatedly writes '1848' on paper and is turned down for a job after accidentally dating his application with that year
Novel: at the end of this novel, the protagonist refuses a free breakfast for the homeless and leaves on a ship bound for England
Novel: in this novel, the protagonist imagines his discovery of the word 'Kuboa' and tries to sell a 'character sketch of Correggio'
Novel: in this novel, the protagonist invents a fairy tale about visiting an octagonal tower in the woods where he sleeps in a vault filled with a million blind angels
Novel: in this novel, the hunter Thomas Glahn lives alone with his dog Aesop and falls in love with the trader's daughter Edvarda
Novel: in this novel, the protagonist murders his dog Aesop and sends the corpse to the woman he loves, Edvarda
Novel: in this novel, the protagonist shoots himself in the left foot to acquire the same limp as his romantic rival
Novel: in this novel, the yellow-suit-wearing Johan Nagel carries a violin case containing dirty laundry and a vial of prussic acid while intriguing a small town
Novel: in this novel, Johan Nagel drowns trying to retrieve a mysterious iron ring he threw into the sea, after berating his doppelganger the 'Midget'
Novel: in this novel, the peasant Isak's wife Inger goes to prison after strangling her newborn daughter, who was born with the same harelip as herself
Author: this Norwegian author wrote a controversial 1945 obituary for Hitler calling him 'a warrior for mankind' and 'a preacher of the gospel of justice for all nations'
Author: after treason charges were dropped due to supposed mental impairment, this author wrote On Overgrown Paths to prove his faculties were intact
Author: this author's essay 'From the Unconscious Life of the Mind' called for novels to attend to 'the whisper of the blood, the pleading of the bone'
Author: Isaac Bashevis Singer called this Norwegian author 'the father of modern literature' and translated many of his works
